topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee in my mug felt suddenly heavy, a physical weight pressing down on my chest. I stared at the enrollment forms, the ink blurring under my gaze. Then I saw it: Mrs. Albright. My daughter, Lily, was in Mrs. Albright’s third-grade class. My own son, Leo, was in the same class. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, pulling at my throat. I reread the names, my hand trembling slightly as I gripped the edge of the desk. How could this be? The sheer improbability of the coincidence made the air in the room feel thin, difficult to draw into my lungs. I pushed the papers away, the sound of the rustling paper loud in the quiet apartment. I needed air.

[story 2]

My fingers kept smoothing the fabric of my sweater, a repetitive, anxious motion. The realization hit me like a sudden drop in temperature. Lily’s school report, tucked under a stack of permission slips, bore the familiar signature: Ms. Davies. My own son, Finn, had been in Ms. Davies’ class last year. The silence in the kitchen was absolute, broken only by the frantic drumming of my pulse against my ribs. I walked to the window, peering out at the street, but nothing seemed to move with any clarity. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. Every small sound—the refrigerator humming, the distant siren—seemed amplified, demanding attention. I needed to talk to someone, but the words wouldn't form, stuck behind a wall of sudden, sharp apprehension.

[story 3]

I kept catching myself glancing at the framed photo of Lily from last summer, the one taken just before she started school. Beside it, the name tag from the classroom roster mocked me. Mrs. Henderson. The same teacher. A strange, prickly sensation crawled up my arms. I paced the length of the living room three times, my footsteps sharp on the hardwood floor. I stopped by the bookshelf, running my fingertips over the spines of old novels, seeking some anchor in the familiar. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. It wasn't just a coincidence; it felt like a deliberate, unsettling alignment of events. I leaned against the cool wood, trying to steady the rapid flutter inside my chest, forcing myself to breathe slowly, deliberately, though the effort felt exhausting.
